summary: This notebook implements a first-place solution for the Image Matching Challenge 2025 by orchestrating a complex, multi-model image matching and 3D reconstruction pipeline. It sets up a custom Python library, symlinks pre-trained vision models, and loads a YAML configuration that defines local feature extractors, descriptor matchers, and a shortlisting ensemble. The pipeline is executed via distributed torchrun to generate a final submission CSV. Readers should understand how to configure and run a unified, multi-stage computer vision workflow using a declarative pipeline system and distributed execution on Kaggle GPUs.
competition_flow: Sets up a custom image matching library and symlinks pre-trained vision models, loads a YAML pipeline configuration defining feature extractors, matchers, and a shortlisting ensemble, then executes the pipeline via distributed torchrun to generate a submission CSV.
data_reading: Data path is set via the DEFAULT_DATASET_DIR environment variable; actual loading and formatting are abstracted by the ns64_imc2025lib library.
data_processing: Pipeline configuration specifies resizing functions (lg_resize: 1280, ml_resize: 1600), NMS radius (4), keypoint thresholds (0.0005), border removal (4), and match thresholds (1.001) for local feature extraction and matching.
ensembling: The shortlist generator uses an ensemble of four retrieval methods (MAST3R-ASMK, MAST3R-SPoC, DINOv2, and ISC) with fallback thresholds and pair-swapping removal to improve matching robustness.
insights: A unified YAML configuration can effectively orchestrate a complex, multi-model image matching and reconstruction pipeline.
Combining local feature matching with global descriptor retrieval improves shortlisting robustness across diverse scenes.
Distributed execution via torchrun is used to accelerate the pipeline on Kaggle GPUs.
